quick_start:
# Your First Working Agent in 48 Hours

This guide assumes zero prior experience with n8n or AI APIs. Follow every step exactly. By the end, you will have a working Customer Support Triage Agent that reads emails, classifies them, and drafts responses.

## Hour 1: Account Setup

**Step 1: Create your n8n Cloud account**
Go to n8n.io. Click 'Start for free.' Create an account with your work email. Verify your email. You'll land in the n8n dashboard — a blank canvas. Don't click anything yet.

**Step 2: Get your Anthropic API key**
Go to console.anthropic.com. Sign up for an account. Navigate to 'API Keys' in the left sidebar. Click 'Create Key.' Name it 'n8n Agent.' Copy the key — it starts with 'sk-ant-api03-'. **Save this somewhere safe — you will not see it again.**

Click 'Billing' and add a payment method. Set a spending limit of $20. This prevents any surprise charges while you're learning.

**Step 3: Set up your agent email address**
In Gmail or Google Workspace, create a label called 'agent-queue.' In Gmail Settings > Filters, create a filter: emails sent to your support address → apply label 'agent-queue.' This is where your agent will watch for new emails.

---

## Hour 2: Connect n8n to Claude

**Step 4: Add your Anthropic credential in n8n**
In n8n, click the puzzle piece icon (Credentials) in the left sidebar. Click 'Add Credential.' Search for 'HTTP Header Auth.' Name it 'Anthropic API.' In the 'Name' field enter: x-api-key. In the 'Value' field, paste your API key. Click Save.

**Step 5: Test that Claude works**
Click 'New Workflow' to create a blank workflow. Click the '+' button in the center of the canvas. Search for 'HTTP Request.' Drag it onto the canvas.

In the node settings:
- Method: POST
- URL: https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages
- Authentication: Header Auth → select your Anthropic API credential
- Send Headers: ON → add header: anthropic-version = 2023-06-01
- Send Body: ON, Body Type: JSON
- JSON Body (paste this exactly):

```json
{
  "model": "claude-haiku-20240307",
  "max_tokens": 100,
  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Say hello in one sentence."}]
}
```

Click the 'Test' button on the node. You should see a green success indicator and a response containing Claude's hello message. If you see a 401 error, your API key is wrong — re-check Step 4. If you see a 400 error, the JSON body has a formatting issue — copy it again exactly.

**You just confirmed Claude is working inside n8n.** This is the foundation everything else builds on.

---

## Hours 3-4: Build Your First Agent

You'll build a simplified version of the Customer Support Triage Agent. Simplified means: one AI step, routes to Slack. Perfect for learning — you add complexity once this works.

**Step 6: Create a new workflow and name it**
Create a new workflow. Click the workflow name at the top and rename it 'Support Triage Agent v1.'

**Step 7: Add the Gmail Trigger**
Click '+' to add a node. Search 'Gmail Trigger.' Select 'New Email.'

Authentication: Click 'Create New' → 'Sign in with Google.' Allow n8n access to Gmail. **Important:** Sign in with your support email account (or personal if testing).

In the node settings:
- Label Filter: select the 'agent-queue' label you created
- Format: Resolved
- Download Attachments: Off

Click 'Test Step' — n8n will ask you to send a test email to your inbox with the agent-queue label applied. Send one. You should see the email data appear in the node output.

**Step 8: Add the Claude Classification node**
Add an HTTP Request node connected to the Gmail trigger. Configure exactly:

- Method: POST
- URL: https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages
- Authentication: your Anthropic credential
- Headers: anthropic-version = 2023-06-01
- Body type: JSON
- JSON Body:

```json
{
  "model": "claude-haiku-20240307",
  "max_tokens": 512,
  "system": "You are a support email classifier. Analyze the email and return ONLY a JSON object: {\"category\": \"BILLING|TECHNICAL|GENERAL|COMPLAINT|SPAM\", \"urgency\": \"LOW|MEDIUM|HIGH\", \"one_line_summary\": \"brief description\"}",
  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Subject: {{ $json.subject }}\nBody: {{ $json.text }}"}]
}
```

Note the `{{ $json.subject }}` syntax — this is how n8n pulls data from the previous node into this one. $json refers to the output of the previous step.

Test this node. You should see a response with Claude's classification JSON.

**Step 9: Add a Code node to parse the JSON**
Add a Code node (search 'Code'). Set language to JavaScript. Paste:

```javascript
const responseText = $input.item.json.content[0].text;
const parsed = JSON.parse(responseText);
return { json: parsed };
```

Test it. You should now see the structured fields: category, urgency, one_line_summary.

**Step 10: Add a Slack notification**
Add a Slack node connected to the Code node.

Authentication: Create New → sign in with Slack, allow permissions.

Settings:
- Resource: Message
- Operation: Post Message
- Channel: paste your Slack channel name (e.g., #support-queue)
- Text:
```
:email: New support email
*From:* {{ $('New Email').item.json.from.value[0].address }}
*Category:* {{ $json.category }}
*Urgency:* {{ $json.urgency }}
*Summary:* {{ $json.one_line_summary }}
```

Test the Slack node. You should see the formatted message appear in your Slack channel.

**Step 11: Activate the workflow**
Toggle the workflow from 'Inactive' to 'Active' using the toggle in the top-right corner of the canvas. The workflow is now live.

Send a test email to your support address with the agent-queue label. Within 60 seconds, you should see a Slack notification with the classification.

**Congratulations. You have a working AI agent.**

---

## Hours 5-8: Improve and Add Auto-Response

Once your basic triage is working, add the auto-response capability.

**Step 12: Add an IF node for routing**
After the Code node, add an IF node. Condition: `{{ $json.category }}` is NOT EQUAL TO 'COMPLAINT' AND `{{ $json.urgency }}` is NOT EQUAL TO 'HIGH'.

True output = auto-respond path. False output = escalation path (the Slack notification you already have).

**Step 13: Add a second Claude node for drafting**
On the True path, add another HTTP Request node (same Claude setup). Update the system prompt to draft a response:

```json
{
  "model": "claude-haiku-20240307",
  "max_tokens": 400,
  "system": "You are a helpful customer support agent. Write a professional, friendly response to this email. Keep it under 150 words. Sign off as 'Support Team.'",
  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Category: {{ $('Parse JSON').item.json.category }}\nOriginal email: {{ $('New Email').item.json.text }}"}]
}
```

**Step 14: Add Gmail Reply node**
Add a Gmail node on the True path:
- Operation: Reply
- Message ID: `{{ $('New Email').item.json.id }}`
- Message: `{{ $json.content[0].text }}`

Test with a real email. The agent should reply automatically to BILLING/TECHNICAL/GENERAL emails and route COMPLAINTs and HIGH urgency to your Slack.

---

## Hour 8-48: Monitor and Refine

For the first 48 hours, manually review every email the agent processes. Check the Gmail 'Sent' folder to see auto-responses. Check Slack for escalations.

For each email, ask: Would I have classified it the same way? Is the auto-response appropriate? If not, note the pattern — then update the system prompt to handle that pattern explicitly.

After 48 hours of monitoring, you'll have a much better system prompt and high confidence in your agent. At that point: reduce manual review to a daily spot-check.

**What to tweak first:**
- If the agent is auto-responding to things it shouldn't, make the COMPLAINT and HIGH urgency rules more sensitive
- If too much is being escalated, the opposite: loosen the escalation criteria
- If responses are too formal or too casual, adjust the tone guidance in the system prompt
- If a common email type isn't being categorized right, add a specific example to the system prompt

You've now built, deployed, and started improving a production AI agent. The pattern you learned here applies to every other agent in this playbook.
about_author:
Sarah Chen is an AI Operations Consultant who has deployed over 60 AI agent systems for businesses ranging from 3-person startups to 200-person companies across industries including e-commerce, professional services, SaaS, real estate, and healthcare administration.

Sarah spent five years in enterprise operations at a Fortune 500 before going independent in 2022, initially helping businesses implement traditional automation (Zapier, Make) and process improvement. When large language models became capable enough for business use in 2023, she pivoted entirely to AI agent deployment and has since become one of the most sought-after non-technical AI operations consultants in the SMB market.

What makes Sarah's approach different: she learned to deploy agents without a coding background. She started with n8n tutorials and API documentation, failed a lot in the first six months, and built a rigorous methodology from what actually worked across dozens of real business deployments. Her clients regularly describe her as the person who 'translates AI hype into things that actually run in my business.'

Notable deployments include: a 12-agent content operations system for a 40-person media company that eliminated 3 full-time contractor positions while increasing output by 40%; a customer service agent network for a 200-person e-commerce brand handling 15,000 emails/month with 72% auto-resolution rate; and a due diligence research agent for a boutique M&A advisory firm that reduced research time per target from 8 hours to 45 minutes.

Sarah runs a monthly live Q&A for purchasers of this playbook and maintains an active update schedule — every quarter, she reviews the tool stack, updates configurations for API changes, and adds new workflow templates based on patterns from current client work.

Her view on the moment we're in: 'Most SMB owners are 18 months behind where they could be because they're waiting for AI to be simpler, or for their developer friends to have time, or for a plug-and-play solution that doesn't exist yet. The tools are here. The barrier is just knowing what to plug in where. That's what this playbook solves.'
